questions.question: How do I stop procrastinating on important tasks?
questions.answer: Procrastination usually stems from fear (of failure, judgment, or the unknown) or perfectionism. Try: (1) Break the task into tiny steps — 'write 200 words' instead of 'write report.' (2) Use the 5-minute rule — commit to just 5 minutes, and you'll usually keep going. (3) Remove friction — prepare everything you need before starting. (4) Set a deadline with accountability — tell someone you'll finish by X time.

questions.question: How do I manage my time when everything feels urgent?
questions.answer: Use the Eisenhower Matrix: (1) Identify what's truly important (not just loud). (2) Ask: 'What happens if I don't do this today?' If the answer is 'nothing significant,' it's not urgent. (3) Communicate timelines: 'I can do this by Thursday — does that work?' (4) Learn to say 'I can do X, but it means Y will be delayed.' This forces others to prioritize.

questions.question: How do I balance work and personal life?
questions.answer: It's not about perfect balance — it's about intentional choices. (1) Set clear boundaries: no work emails after 7 PM. (2) Schedule personal time like meetings — exercise, family dinners, hobbies. (3) Protect your 'off' time fiercely. (4) Remember: you can have everything, just not all at once. Some seasons require more work; others require more rest.

questions.question: What's the most common time management mistake?
questions.answer: Confusing being busy with being productive. Most people fill their day with low-value tasks (email, meetings, administrative work) and call it productivity. Real productivity means doing fewer things but higher-impact things. Ask yourself: 'If I could only do ONE thing today, what would create the most value?' Do that first.

questions.question: How do I handle interruptions and distractions?
questions.answer: Three strategies: (1) Prevent them — close your door, put your phone in another room, use 'do not disturb' mode. (2) Batch them — tell people you check messages at 10 AM and 3 PM only. (3) Manage them — when interrupted, say 'I'm in the middle of something. Can I come find you in 30 minutes?' This respects their need while protecting your focus.
